speaker: interviewer
text: --six. The survivor is Mr. Joseph Greenblatt, tape two. So you came back to Warsaw after you had been in-- in-- you had been finished with all your military training, and you came back. Did you start practicing law, working in the law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Start practicing in this, because you see, what we had to clerk for-- for an attorney for three years. So I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got a position.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the position.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your boss attorney a Jewish attorney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish. Yeshua--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you have gone to another--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeshua Halpen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you work for a non-Jewish attorney?

speaker: interviewee
text: If he would take me. I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they wouldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had Yeshua Halpen, which was actually off record Begin's brother-in-law. And we were party affiliates.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you worked for a salary, or was that a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was basically--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pocket money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pocket money. But it didn't last long, was I was soon mobilized.

speaker: interviewer
text: When where you mobilized?

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I think it was June of 1938. Mobilized for the conflict at this-- with the-- with the-- with-- with Czechoslovakia. [INAUDIBLE]. That's where I was-- what I did by law, when you are mobilized not for basic training so-- or recalled for maneuvers. You have to be upgraded one-- one-- one, this, position. So I was upgraded to a second lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a-- a uniform of the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I got money from the-- from the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Government.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the government to buy myself two uniforms, one field uniform, one parade uniform. Had to buy my-- to buy a gun, to buy shoes, for field shoes and parade shoes. And the belts, everything. But after the graduation. After I was nominated as a first lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so--

speaker: interviewee
text: Second lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have to report any place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every year for four months-- for four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I didn't have so much time because in soon I guess-- soon I came back from this-- from Czechoslovakia, I was mobilized already in April, end of April, for World War II with the-- with the silent--

speaker: interviewer
text: In April '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For the silent mobilization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know why you were being mobilized?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. We knew that the war is hanging on-- on a hair. And at anytime. And we were transported in-- in June already to the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish-German border. We were stationed about well, less than 10 kilometers from the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think anything of this magnitude was going to come from the conflict?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We figured out, oh, goes, ah, will be like a soldier in Czechoslovakia and nothing will happen. They'll come to a-- an agreement and we-- then we all whatever telling is Polish British agreement, that they will give us the-- supply the airplanes and bombers and everything. And from France, the same thing. Of course, that didn't come out from that. And they were lying to us through their teeth. And the war broke out. We were about three kilometers already from the German border, because we moved up. But then we-- the retreat started right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the army was disorganized?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We couldn't withstand the-- the bombardment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were overpowered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At the daytime, we had the air force, the German Air Force. At night, we had the-- the guerrillas. The Volksdeutschen. Sitting on a tree what we called the-- the pigeons. And they were shooting at us and burning all-- the whole-- burning all-- whole villages, all-- all this. The-- the fields were on fire, which were make harder to-- to retreat. Because all the sabotage was behind our backs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Till we catch up-- till the-- at 18 day of war, I was taken prisoner of war. I was wounded. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was taken prisoner of war. That was under that famous Kessel. I don't know how you say Kessel in-- in English. I always wondering how-- how do you-- it's a pot. A big pot.

speaker: interviewer
text: A kettle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But the Kessel in-- in military means surrounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were surrounded by what they did. That's-- we were trying to get through from [POLISH] over the river to-- to Warsaw, and that's when we were surrounded and taken prisoner of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: First we were taken to a field outside-- not far from [POLISH] to [POLISH], Mazowiecki. And there, we were on a sports field. There, they started to separate officers from enlisted men. And then we walked to-- were transported after two days. Hunger. Rainy weather. It was the fall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were transported to Dessau, Germany. To--

speaker: interviewer
text: How? On a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We walked. For three days we walked. Ah, I remember one episode on that march-- not such a pleasant one, but I'll tell you anyhow. We are adults now. We can talk its best. What we ate on that field, potato peelings. We cooked it. We ate it. And on the march at night, I got cramps my stomach. And I couldn't-- I could-- I was such a cramps, I didn't see where I was going. And we were thousands of people in the column. And my friends in the-- in the side said to me, Joe, or Joseph, pull down your pants. We'll split until they come to the end. You're still in the column. And that's what I-- and that's how I relieved myself. It was a horrible feeling. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In my life, I didn't go through another episode like that. Finally, when I finish, forget about cleaning yourself. I mean, with what? And I was little better, caught up with my-- Group.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with my group, and I continued walking with them. They didn't know I'm a Jew. I didn't know who they are-- who they were. If it was from division, or who care. Till we were transported by train. I don't know what-- what town or what city we reached. We were put in a cattle car and moved to-- to Germany, to Amitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amitz. M-I-- A-M-I-T-Z-. Amitz Kreis Gruben. And there, then I went to camp. I was wounded, so they-- they kept me for a while in the lazarette to clean-- till the wound healed, and on my bed in the camp was Ober Lieutenant Josef Israel Greenblatt. Israel was for Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every Jew got the name Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Officers and every Jew in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The others were either way separating Jews and non-Jews, the enlisted men. We were in barracks of seven. Some officers. We were supposed to be-- I was supposed to be with the younger officers, because officers are divided in three categories. Young office, senior officers, colonels, then it was generals.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was among the senior officers because there was no room. And over there, after a while when I was released from the-- this-- from the lazarette. Hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the hospital, lazarette, whatever they call-- they call it lazarette.

speaker: interviewer
text: Field hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Field hospital. I had to go into a barrack with the-- with captains. And over there was planned an escape. And they took me in the confidence. They knew I'm a Jew because it said on my bed who I am. I was very well introduced. [LAUGHS] And the others put me in the confidence. And after seven months, finally we escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you. During those seven months in-- in-- you were taken captive, were you able to communicate with your family at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did give us postcards to mail.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. According to my family after my return to Warsaw, they received one. I mail about five. My parents wrote back to me. I didn't get any. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The escape-- So tell me about your escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: The escape was with the help of the Polish underground. That time, the [SPEAKING POLISH], the-- the army of the land-- in pure translation that's what it means-- was not yet a combat unit. They call it civil disobedience. And since among the captains in my [INAUDIBLE], there were relatives of high ranking generals of Poland. So they got the contact, and they could-- somehow through this way the Polish underground was conducted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the escape? We-- in the camp, there were wild cherries growing. And we're chewing the cherries. And whenever we pass by a-- a watchman or a [GERMAN], we started to cough. And blood came out, blood red cherries are.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were afraid it's TB, and they sent us to quarantine. And it was in Pozna, which is a-- a city which was on the German border, and it was annexed to Germany. When we reached the outskirts of Pozna, the-- the-- this-- we went by truck. The truck was attacked. Polish underground went, and we were liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't easy. Smuggled ourself through because we all-- but we had the uniforms. Finally we got-- anyway, when we reached the territorial Poland, which is not far from Pozna, it's the outskirt of Pozna, the city of-- what's the name. [POLISH]. We got some civil clothes and put on the civil clothes, and we smuggled themselves out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came back to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came back to my--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The 19th of-- 12th of March of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find in your family's home in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all I found out my brother-in-law was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Already.

speaker: interviewee
text: By By bombing. When they-- well, you see, that the president of the city of Warsaw asked the men to leave Warsaw, go east. We don't know if there was a sellout of Warsaw, whatever. So the men started to walk. But meantime, they were bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your sister's husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he was killed in this, in-- can't remember the moment, that army. He was-- where he was killed. He was with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your sister living in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home, my sister was living back in my parents' house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember when I came, it was the middle of the night. Was again smuggling ourself. It was a curfew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know there was a curfew already? Did you know that the situation was very different--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Not for the Jews. It was a curfew. No one could walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: War curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it wasn't the ghetto yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow we smuggled-- I smuggled myself with another fellow. From one building to another one, knowing Warsaw like the palm of my hand. I walked through, and reached the-- this-- then we separate a few blocks away because he went his way and I went my way. And I didn't go to my parents' house. My brother lived in next building, Smolna 9.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was married. He lived at Smolna 9. And I rang over there. I didn't want to disturb my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The super knew me over there, and he took me in. Let me in. He helped me to go up to-- to my parents' house when I said to him I was just released from the prisoner of war camp. And went up to-- to-- to them, and they had already-- my brother had his in-laws living with him because their house was bombed. In the morning, they called it. Still they had telephone. Tell my father that a client is in their house. They would like him to come up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father came up, and you can imagine--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] --when he saw me. Because he had in his mind that I lost a leg. Because a friend who was discharged [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] told him-- told my father, Joe is OK. Only he lost a leg--

speaker: interviewer
text: Leg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Injured.

speaker: interviewee
text: --injured here. And because he lost two arms, same fellow. He went to-- my father-- first thing, clamped about my legs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I went down to my father's house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And first thing I saw the night here. First thing next morning, first of all, I-- when I came home, I was-- my weight was about 49 kilo. And I was-- had a cold from traveling, because it-- a few days it took us to get to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I got the arm band from my father. If you go out, remember we all have the wear the arm band.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, every Jew had to wear an armband.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the arm band.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which color?

speaker: interviewee
text: White with a blue--

speaker: interviewer
text: Star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: --star of David. And then I walked a little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while And I saw they're building a wall. High, high wall. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: This had several feet high, several meters high.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cement wall?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a brick wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brick wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brick wall, with cement topped with glass and-- and this barbed wire, with a big sign in German. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And underneath in Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH]. And in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Same thing in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was already in March. Then it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Father still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Because there was no import. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was selling out what was in stock. But no-- no-- no new thing were coming in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was food rare?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was food scarce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was rationed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Already.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was different rations for Jews, different rations for-- for non-Jews, and different nations for Volksdeutsche. They had different stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then what happened? You couldn't get a job anymore. There was nothing for you to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Had to live from-- for what existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were still living in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At that time, we were still living in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk of leaving? Was there any way one could have left? Or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You could-- only thing, you could smuggle yourself into Russia, the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, not-- not-- don't want to mistake that the Soviet Jew-- Russian, or the Soviet Union, ever saved Jews. They did not. Only the Jews smuggled themselves in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the Soviet Union. And then they were sent to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those-- if the River Bug could talk, hundreds of hundreds and maybe of thousands were drowned over there, which the subbers were shotting at them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So what did you do? You stayed in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, my friends on Betar came to me not long. They say few days after I came back, it was known that I am-- I am back.

speaker: interviewer
text: In town again.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to me and told me, you know, the Betar still is in existence. We are illegal, but we still meet, and those who belonged to the former-- to the-- to the Corridor, what we call the [HEBREW] are meeting. You are a member of the Corridor, so you're welcome to join us back. We made an appointment for two days later, and I was taken by Paul Frankel who was in charge of the-- of the [HEBREW] Corridor. So-called [SPEAKING HEBREW], if you understand Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was taken to a house in the strictly Jewish neighborhood, [INAUDIBLE] 23. Went into that house. From that house, we went into a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how is it-- usually it's a [HEBREW] and a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A shelter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: A cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. On the top of the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: The attic. That is, through that attic, we went into another attic for another building. Then we went through about two floors, and went in through a broken wall to another building. Went up to the attic, then to another three attics, and then we went down--

speaker: interviewer
text: You met.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I was taken down to a arsenal. And my eyes didn't want to believe what was there. Heavy machine guns. Grenades. Rifles. Guns. Sables. Bayonets. You name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to Paul, Paul. How, from where? He said, when the Polish Army surrendered, they were throwing their arms in the middle of the street. And our boys went out-- And picked it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pick them up. But the-- the war will take six months, another half a year, maybe a year. We'll have more to send to the [INAUDIBLE]. To Palestine. That was an arsenal. And that but was the-- I would say the beginning of the arms collection for the revolt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like I said to you, time after the war there were two organizations, the Jewish fighting organization, which led by Mordechai Anielewicz, and the Jewish military organization, which was led by Paul Frankel. I became the-- the military instructor because of my military background. There weren't too many officers, Jewish officers, actually. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In Ringelblum's memoirs, he speaks about the Jewish military organization. And he says it was a smaller group, but armed to their teeth. The best armed group, and we proved it in the revolt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came. Then it was-- we were told-- my-- my parents told me, actually. Joe, be careful. Don't go out, because they are gripping to-- this to-- to slap awake. You'll be-- they-- they're taking it to-- to the park of the Polish parliament. And they're digging graves over there where they are shotting people. They were picking people from the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Randomly?

speaker: interviewee
text: At random. They-- they saw the armband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, to dig the--the graves for those who they-- whom they shot the day before, the night before. And there over in that park, they were burying them. That's what they take, the Jews. And my father, that's not for you. You go through enough. Saw enough corpses already. I didn't realize this-- but this was the beginning, the corpses. And we organized also as civil disobedience group. We had patrol-- we were patrolling the streets and telling the Jews, don't go here. The-- the trucks are there. Don't go this way. The trucks are there. Go-- safer to go this way. That was our main task till we went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you have to move into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was November 15 of 1940. That's the-- the day when the ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was established.

speaker: interviewee
text: --gates were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody had to be in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which ghetto-- did they assign you a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, was one ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was one ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: One ghetto. Then they started to cut and it became a small ghetto, a big ghetto, and a normal slant. I was in-- in the central ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you take things along? Were you allowed to take anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you could carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: No wagons, no--

speaker: interviewee
text: NO. A pushcart. Then it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --also a voluntary-- they-- because the decree for going to the ghetto was announced on-- just before Neilah on Yom Kippur. The whole day, we knew only some news was coming this afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to the ghetto, did you tell non-Jews to go into your place? Did you hide anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I was aiming to tell you. It was a voluntary exchange of apartments. The non-Jews who were living in the Jewish quarters could exchange apartments. But the demands for them, before they were-- before they were walking, living in an attic without the toilet, without any. Now they want a two-- two bathrooms and with this, and a three bedroom apartment or four bedroom apartment, in the most elaborate section of Warsaw. But it-- some-- it was accommodated. There-- that's why it was postponed for two weeks, the moving. From the 1st of November to the 15th. Because it was such a nice exchange between the Poles and the Jews. Our apartment, a neighbor who was flirting with the-- with the-- with the Nazis, a woman, ripped our apartment-- our apartment. And my father was beaten up that time, only she didn't let them touch me under no cost. For them, I was still a Polish officer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took our apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my wife's sister, the one which is in Australia, lived in our building on Smolna, and her sister, which I showed you, it was my most beloved sister-in-law, who lived on Smolna 14. And she told me, you know, my neighbor-- my sister's neighbor has-- wants to split her apartment with someone, so you better go and grab it. So we got that apartment, two rooms in her-- in her apartment. And that's how I actually started to flirt with my wife, because she was next door-- her-- with her sister. She moved in to her sister. That's how he met. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had two rooms for your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: For yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: --myself, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister. Your widowed sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My widowed sister. And my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but a very short--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: --very short time. They found-- for a very short time. All to have eight people in two rooms was very common. It was very common to have eight people in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bathroom to use? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And kitchen facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We could use the kitchen facilities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other families sharing the same kitchen and bathroom with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The-- the owners of the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the owners.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a woman with her son. It was a widow woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Strangers.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yes. And my sister-- sister-in-law introduced us, and that's quite common housing stuff. Was my neighbor introduced me to--to her sister. That's-- because we were-- we were very close, and give me a-- give us a good reference, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the conditions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't call it even they were conditions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's typhoid, starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away. Early on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew right away it's-- because people came in with nothing. Food was scarce. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those days were-- in the beginning, they were smuggling a little bit. We had some friends who were smuggling to us. And good friends. One was even a Volksdeutsche. Only he didn't want to list himself as a Volksdeutsche. He was a butcher. And he was bringing me-- bringing us-- of course, we were completely treyf in that time. [BACKGROUND NOISE]
